    <PageDescription mdxType="PageDescription">
      <p>{`The `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`elaunch.py`}</inlineCode>{` command line tool executes and monitors virtual experiments. You can use it to run experiments on your laptop or on a High Performance Computing Cluster. When you submit a virtual experiment via ST4SD API it is executed by `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`elaunch.py`}</inlineCode>{`.`}</p>
    </PageDescription>
    <h2>{`Install elaunch.py`}</h2>
    <p>{`If you haven’t already, install the `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`st4sd-runtime-core`}</inlineCode>{` python package:`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{}}>{`pip install "st4sd-runtime-core[develop]>=2.5.1"
`}</code></pre>
    <h2>{`Running experiments with elaunch.py`}</h2>
    <p>{`Use elaunch.py to run virtual experiments: `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`elaunch.py <path to experiment>`}</inlineCode>{`.`}</p>
    <p>{`Note, running experiments locally requires using either Linux or MacOS.
Windows users should either use a Virtual Machine (e.g. `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "https://www.virtualbox.org"
      }}>{`VirtualBox`}</a>{` etc) or the Windows Subsystem for Linux (`}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install"
      }}>{`WSL`}</a>{`).`}</p>
    <p>{`Some experiments, like the one in this example, also require you to install one of `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "https://docs.docker.com/engine/install/"
      }}>{`docker`}</a>{`, `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "https://podman.io/docs/installation"
      }}>{`podman`}</a>{`, or `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "https://docs.rancherdesktop.io/getting-started/installation/"
      }}>{`Rancher Desktop`}</a>{`.`}</p>
    <p>{`In this example you will run the workflow `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "https://registry.st4sd.res.ibm.com/registry-ui/experiment/nanopore-geometry-experiment"
      }}><inlineCode parentName="a">{`nanopore-geometry-experiment`}</inlineCode></a>{` using docker. It consists of the following computational graph:`}</p>
    <Column colMd={4} colLg={8} noGutterSm mdxType="Column">

    <p>{`To execute it first download its code using `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`git`}</inlineCode>{`:`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-bash"
      }}>{`git clone https://github.com/st4sd/nanopore-geometry-experiment.git
cd nanopore-geometry-experiment

`}</code></pre>
    <p>{`Then run elaunch.py specifying certain files in the directory created above:`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-bash"
      }}>{`elaunch.py -i docker-example/cif_files.dat -l 40 --nostamp\\
      --applicationDependencySource="nanopore-database=cif:copy" \\
      nanopore-geometry-experiment.package
`}</code></pre>
    <p>{`The experiment should take about 5 minutes to complete. The `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`-l 40`}</inlineCode>{` option keeps the log printouts to the bare minimum so don’t worry if the command is silent for a few minutes. When the experiment completes expect to see something similar to the following text on your terminal:`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{}}>{`completed-on=2024-03-15 14:39:29.909105
cost=0
created-on=2024-03-15 14:38:04.402969
current-stage=stage3
exit-status=Success
experiment-state=finished
stage-progress=1.0
stage-state=finished
stages=['stage0', 'stage1', 'stage2', 'stage3']
total-progress=1.0
updated=2024-03-15 14:39:33.804727
updated-on=2024-03-15 14:39:33.804727
`}</code></pre>
    <p>{`If the experiment works `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`elaunch.py`}</inlineCode>{` prints `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`exit-status=Success`}</inlineCode>{` before it terminates and then exits with return code 0. You can find more information about the status of your experiment under the file `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`\${package_name}-\${timestamp}.instance/output/status.txt`}</inlineCode>{`. For example, here’s a `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`status.txt`}</inlineCode>{` for a successful run of an experiment:`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{}}>{`completed-on=2025-03-21 13:15:19.790371
cost=0

updated=2025-03-21 13:15:19.799143
updated-on=2025-03-21 13:15:19.799143
`}</code></pre>
    <h2>{`What is the output of my experiment ?`}</h2>
    <p>{`Looking inside the instance directory created for the experiment, we see the following:`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{}}>{`ls -lth nanopore-geometry-experiment.instance
total 64
drwxrwxr-x@ 10 user  wheel   320B 21 Mar 13:15 output/

drwxr-xr-x@  4 user  wheel   128B 21 Mar 13:03 nanopore-database/
`}</code></pre>
    <h4>{`Where can I see what steps were executed during the experiment?`}</h4>
    <p>{`The `}<strong parentName="p"><inlineCode parentName="strong">{`stages`}</inlineCode></strong>{` directory contains the working directories for each step executed during this experiment instance. A `}<em parentName="p">{`stage`}</em>{` represents a logical grouping of components within a workflow. Most experiments include a single stage named `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`stage0`}</inlineCode>{`, but in this case, there are three: `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`stage0`}</inlineCode>{`, `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`stage1`}</inlineCode>{`, and `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`stage2`}</inlineCode>{`.`}</p>
    <p>{`Each working directory includes the files generated by a component, along with its standard output and error logs. Examining these directories is helpful for debugging or understanding how each component behaves. For example, take a look inside `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`stages/stage1/Zeo0`}</inlineCode>{` to see the files produced by the `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`Zeo`}</inlineCode>{` component. The trailing `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`0`}</inlineCode>{` indicates that only one instance of this component was run.`}</p>
    <p>{`We’ll cover component replication and stages in different tutorials, so you don’t need to worry about those concepts just yet.`}</p>
    <h4>{`Where can I find the properties that my experiment measured ?`}</h4>
    <p>{`The `}<strong parentName="p"><inlineCode parentName="strong">{`output`}</inlineCode></strong>{` directory contains the measured properties of the input systems that were processed, along with metadata about the key outputs of your experiment.`}</p>
    <p>{`Some experiments define an `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`interface`}</inlineCode>{` that specifies which properties are measured for each system. The `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`nanopore-geometry-experiment`}</inlineCode>{` is one such example. You can find the measured properties in the file `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`\${instance-directory}/output/properties.csv`}</inlineCode>{`.`}</p>
    <p>{`Here is an example of the output generated by `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`nanopore-geometry-experiment`}</inlineCode>{`:`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{}}>{`input-id;d_is;d_fs;d_isfs;asa_m^2/cm^3;asa_m^2/g;nasa_m^2/cm^3;nasa_m^2/g;unitcell_volume;density;av_volume_fraction;av_cm^3/g;nav_volume_fraction;nav_cm^3/g;n_pockets
CoRE2019/GUJVOX_clean;3.98106;2.74095;3.93078;0.0;0.0;760.654;564.703;1164.59;1.347;0.0;0.0;0.016015;0.0118894;1.0
`}</code></pre>
    <p>{`The CSV file always includes an `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`input-id`}</inlineCode>{` column, which identifies the input systems for which properties were measured. The remaining columns list the specific properties measured for each system.`}</p>
